stem: How is the echo formed in a basic gradient-echo sequence?

options.text: By applying a 180-degree refocusing RF pulse
By waiting for complete T1 recovery
By switching off the main magnetic field
By reversing a dephasing gradient to rephase transverse magnetization

explanation: Gradient echo uses gradient polarity reversal rather than a 180-degree RF pulse to bring spins back into phase. This permits shorter TR and TE values.

stem: Why is gradient-echo signal described by T2-star rather than true T2?

options.text: Static field inhomogeneity is not refocused by a 180-degree pulse
Spin-spin interactions are completely eliminated by gradient reversal
Longitudinal recovery is measured without transverse signal
Receiver bandwidth is always zero

explanation: Gradient reversal refocuses gradient-induced phase but does not remove dephasing from susceptibility and B0 variation. The measured decay therefore includes both T2 and inhomogeneity effects.

stem: Which feature allows gradient-echo sequences to use very short repetition times?

options.text: A mandatory 180-degree pulse restores all longitudinal magnetization
Small flip angles leave more longitudinal magnetization available for repeated excitation
Every tissue completes T1 recovery before the next pulse
Phase encoding is unnecessary in gradient echo

explanation: A partial flip uses only part of the longitudinal magnetization, limiting saturation during rapid repetitions. Omitting the 180-degree pulse also reduces RF energy and sequence duration.

stem: Compared with a similar spin-echo acquisition, why can gradient echo have lower RF power deposition?

options.text: It uses a stronger static magnetic field for every patient
It eliminates all RF excitation
It omits the repeated 180-degree refocusing pulses
It requires twice as many phase-encoding steps

explanation: High-flip-angle refocusing trains contribute substantially to specific absorption rate. Gradient echo generally uses one lower-flip excitation and gradient-based rephasing.

stem: Which finding is gradient-echo imaging especially sensitive to?

options.text: Only longitudinal recovery in uniform water
Complete cancellation of all off-resonance effects
Slice wrap caused solely by a long TR
Local susceptibility differences from blood products or metal

explanation: Without a 180-degree refocusing pulse, local field variations cause prominent signal loss and blooming. This sensitivity is useful for blood products but can be problematic near metal or air.
